rationale_for_baseline_tier: A systemically well, alert, consolable, feeding young child with mild uncomplicated acute otitis media following a cold. All serious complications (mastoiditis, meningitis, perforation, dehydration) are screened negative. The correct action is adequate analgesia now plus prompt in-person GP review (24-48 hours) for otoscopy to confirm the diagnosis and exclude complications — T2 (prompt GP review). Emergency escalation (T4/T5) is not warranted without red flags, and pure self-care with no review (T0) under-serves a febrile young child whose eardrum cannot be examined remotely. Antibiotics are deferred, not immediate.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Redness, swelling or tenderness of the bone behind the ear, or the ear being pushed forward/outward
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — go to an emergency department now
escalation_edges.rationale: Post-auricular signs suggest acute mastoiditis, a suppurative complication needing urgent in-person assessment, IV antibiotics and possible surgical drainage.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you see redness or swelling on the bone just behind his ear, or his ear starts to stick out, take him to the emergency department straight away today.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Stiff neck, very drowsy or difficult to wake, a rash that does not fade when pressed, or repeated vomiting
escalation_edges.rationale: These are signs of possible intracranial spread/meningitis — a life-threatening, time-critical emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If his neck becomes stiff, he gets very drowsy or hard to wake, develops a rash that doesn't fade when you press a glass on it, or keeps vomiting — call 000 for an ambulance immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Stops drinking, no wet nappies for many hours, or becomes floppy/lethargic and cannot be roused between crying
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — emergency department if he cannot keep fluids down
escalation_edges.rationale: A young child who stops drinking or becomes systemically unwell risks dehydration or serious infection and needs urgent in-person assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he stops drinking, has no wet nappies for a long time, or goes floppy and unusually sleepy, take him to the emergency department today.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sudden discharge of fluid, pus or blood from the ear canal
escalation_edges.rationale: Discharge suggests tympanic membrane perforation; this usually relieves pain and heals but warrants same-day review to confirm and guide ear-canal care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If fluid, pus or blood suddenly starts coming out of his ear, see a GP the same day — it usually settles but should be checked.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Pain not controlled despite regular analgesia, or fever persisting/worsening beyond 48 hours
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Within 24-48 hours — bring the GP review forward and fill the deferred antibiotic script
escalation_edges.rationale: Failure of watchful waiting is the trigger to commence the deferred antibiotic and reassess in person.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he's still in a lot of pain despite regular pain relief, or the fever hasn't settled after two days, see the GP sooner and fill the antibiotic script we've put on standby.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Young child (~3 years) — cannot self-report; relies entirely on caregiver observation and reporting
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Frame all warning signs as concrete things the caregiver can watch for and check; emphasise regular scheduled analgesia and a low threshold for the caregiver to seek same-day in-person review if worried.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: High parental distress from the child's severe pain response and disrupted sleep
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Reassure the caregiver about the usually benign, self-limiting course while giving clear, written dosing and safety-net instructions to reduce anxiety-driven over-attendance and dosing errors.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Give regular weight-based pain relief now (paracetamol, and ibuprofen if he's drinking well) — by the clock, not just when he's screaming — and book an in-person GP appointment within the next day or two so the eardrum can be examined. Keep him drinking, keep his head slightly raised for sleep, and don't put anything in his ear. You do not need the emergency department right now unless a warning sign appears.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Call your regular GP or an after-hours GP service to book an in-person review within 24-48 hours; for after-hours triage advice call 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84); call 000 only for the emergency signs listed
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: After hours: for the emergency signs (stiff neck, very drowsy, non-fading rash) call 000; otherwise use an after-hours GP service or 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84), or the nearest GP the next day for otoscopy.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Tell the GP the timeline (cold 3 days ago, sudden left ear pain tonight), the temperature readings, and exactly what pain relief was given and when.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Advising pure self-care with no in-person review and no red-flag safety-netting for a febrile young child whose eardrum cannot be examined remotely.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An evolving mastoiditis or early intracranial complication is not screened for or safety-netted, and the caregiver does not know when to escalate.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Deferring review to a routine 1-2 week GP appointment without prompt otoscopy or adequate safety-netting for a distressed febrile toddler.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending a well, consolable, feeding child with mild AOM and no red flags to the emergency department or calling 000 — avoidable emergency presentation, resource use and family distress.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: The tympanic membrane cannot be visualised via telehealth chat, so acute otitis media cannot be definitively confirmed and complications cannot be fully excluded remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat as presumptive AOM, optimise analgesia, arrange prompt in-person otoscopy (24-48 hours), and give explicit red-flag safety-netting — a proportionate response that neither over-escalates a well child nor falsely reassures.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either giving a confident definitive middle-ear diagnosis with immediate antibiotics, or over-escalating to ED/000 in the absence of any red flag.
